statement: if y >= 1 and x > 0 and y >= 4 x ln(x), then y >= x ln(2y)
If 0 < x ≤ 1 and y ≥ 1, then y ≥ x ln(2y).
proof: Since y ≥ 1, we have 2y ≥ 2, so ln(2y) ≥ ln 2 > 0. Because x ≤ 1 and ln(2y) ≥ 0, multiplying the inequality x ≤ 1 by the non‑negative number ln(2y) gives x ln(2y) ≤ ln(2y). Define h(y) = y − ln(2y) for y ≥ 1. Then h′(y) = 1 − 1/y ≥ 0 (since y ≥ 1), so h is non‑decreasing. Evaluating at y = 1 yields h(1) = 1 − ln 2 > 0. Hence for all y ≥ 1, h(y) ≥ h(1) > 0, which means y > ln(2y); in particular y ≥ ln(2y). Combining the two inequalities we obtain y ≥ ln(2y) ≥ x ln(2y). Therefore y ≥ x ln(2y).

statement: For every x > 1, we have 4x ln x ≥ x ln(8x ln x).
proof: Divide both sides by x > 0 to obtain the equivalent inequality 4 ln x ≥ ln(8x ln x) = ln 8 + ln x + ln ln x, i.e., 3 ln x ≥ ln 8 + ln ln x. Define h(x) = 3 ln x − ln 8 − ln ln x for x > 1. Then h′(x) = 3/x − 1/(x ln x) = (3 ln x − 1)/(x ln x). Since the denominator is positive for x > 1, the sign of h′ is determined by the numerator. Thus h is decreasing on (1, e^{1/3}) and increasing on (e^{1/3}, ∞); its global minimum occurs at x = e^{1/3}. Compute h(e^{1/3}) = 3·(1/3) − ln 8 − ln(1/3) = 1 − ln 8 + ln 3 = 1 − ln(8/3). Because 8/3 < e, we have ln(8/3) < 1, therefore h(e^{1/3}) > 0. Consequently h(x) > 0 for all x > 1, which means 3 ln x > ln 8 + ln ln x. Multiplying by x yields 4x ln x > x ln(8x ln x); in particular, the non‑strict inequality 4x ln x ≥ x ln(8x ln x) holds.

statement: For any real numbers x > 1 and y ≥ 1, if y ≥ 4x ln x, then y ≥ x ln(2y).
proof:
Define f(y) = y − x ln(2y). Then f′(y) = 1 − x/y. Assume y ≥ 4x ln x.

Case 1: 4x ln x ≥ x, i.e., x ≥ e^{1/4}. Then y ≥ x, so f′(y) ≥ 0; thus f is non‑decreasing on [4x ln x, ∞). Hence f(y) ≥ f(4x ln x) = 4x ln x − x ln(8x ln x). By the established inequality (for every x > 1, 4x ln x ≥ x ln(8x ln x)) this quantity is non‑negative, so f(y) ≥ 0, i.e., y ≥ x ln(2y).

Case 2: 4x ln x < x, i.e., 1 < x < e^{1/4}. Then x < e/2 (since e^{1/4} < e/2), therefore ln(2x) ≤ 1 and f(x) = x − x ln(2x) = x(1 − ln(2x)) ≥ 0. On [4x ln x, x] we have y ≤ x, so f′(y) = 1 − x/y ≤ 0 and f is non‑increasing; for y ≥ x we have f′(y) ≥ 0 and f is non‑decreasing. Consequently the minimum of f on [4x ln x, ∞) occurs at y = x. Because f(x) ≥ 0, we obtain f(y) ≥ 0 for all y ≥ 4x ln x, which is exactly y ≥ x ln(2y).

Both cases yield the desired implication, completing the proof.

statement: For any real numbers x > 0 and y ≥ 1, if y ≥ 4x ln(x), then y ≥ x ln(2y).
proof:
We consider two cases.
1. 0 < x ≤ 1. By the first established statement, for any such x and any y ≥ 1 we have y ≥ x ln(2y). The hypothesis y ≥ 4x ln(x) is automatically true because 4x ln(x) ≤ 0 ≤ y, but it is not needed; the conclusion holds regardless.
2. x > 1. The third established statement directly asserts that for x > 1 and y ≥ 1, the implication y ≥ 4x ln(x) ⇒ y ≥ x ln(2y) holds.
Thus the implication is valid for all x > 0, y ≥ 1.
